title: Drip vs Flodesk (2025): Which Is Better?
meta_description: Compare Drip and Flodesk email marketing tools. See pricing, features, and which is best for your business needs.
intro: Drip and Flodesk are both popular email marketing platforms, but they serve different audiences and use cases. Drip specializes in e-commerce automation with deep store integration, while Flodesk prioritizes beautiful design and simplicity for visual brands. This comparison will help you determine which platform aligns best with your business goals.

comparison_table.tool_a: E-commerce businesses with complex automation needs

comparison_table.tool_b: Stunning templates, unlimited subscribers, design tools

choose_a_if: You run an e-commerce store and need deep integration with your sales platform
Revenue attribution and detailed purchase tracking are critical for your business
You want to combine email with SMS marketing in one platform
choose_b_if: You're a designer, photographer, or creative professional who needs beautiful email templates
You have a large subscriber list and want unlimited contacts at a flat rate
You prioritize intuitive design and user experience over advanced automation features

faq.a: Neither is universally better—it depends on your needs. Drip excels for e-commerce businesses needing advanced automation and revenue tracking, while Flodesk wins for design-focused brands valuing aesthetics and simplicity. Flodesk has higher customer satisfaction ratings overall.

faq.q: Which is cheaper, Drip or Flodesk?
faq.a: Pricing is nearly identical at the starting point ($38-39/month), but costs diverge based on subscriber count. Drip charges per subscriber, so costs scale with growth, while Flodesk's flat-rate model offers unlimited subscribers at a fixed price, making it cheaper for larger lists.

faq.q: Can I switch from Flodesk to Drip?
faq.a: Yes, most email platforms allow you to export your subscriber lists and campaign history. Both Flodesk and Drip support standard export formats. However, you'll need to recreate automations and workflows manually, as they don't have direct migration tools between them.
